    color: #000000 !important;
}

#onetrust-pc-sdk #ot-pc-desc a {
    margin-left: 0px;
    display: inline;
    color: inherit;
    text-decoration: underline;
    text-decoration-color: currentcolor;
    text-decoration-thickness: auto;
    -webkit-text-decoration-color: #76b900;
    text-decoration-color: #76b900;
    text-decoration-thickness: 2px;
    text-underline-offset: .3125em;
}
#onetrust-pc-sdk #ot-pc-desc a:hover {
    text-decoration-color: #000000;
}
#onetrust-consent-sdk #onetrust-pc-sdk #ot-pc-desc {
    font-size: 15px;
}

#onetrust-consent-sdk #onetrust-pc-sdk .category-host-list-handler {
    color: #000;
    padding-bottom: 2px;
    border-bottom: 2px solid #76b900;
    text-decoration: none;
    letter-spacing: normal;
    font-size: 15px;
    padding-top: 15px;
}

#onetrust-consent-sdk #onetrust-pc-sdk .category-host-list-handler:hover {
    border-bottom: 2px solid #000;
}

#onetrust-pc-sdk .ot-tgl input:checked+.ot-switch .ot-switch-nob {
    background-color: #76b900;
}

#onetrust-pc-sdk #filter-btn-handler {
    background-color: #76b900;
    border: 1px solid #76b900;
    opacity: 1;
}
#onetrust-pc-sdk #filter-btn-handler:hover {
    background-color: #91c733;
    border: 1px solid #91c733;
}

#onetrust-pc-sdk .ot-pc-footer .ot-btn-container button.ot-pc-refuse-all-handler,
#onetrust-pc-sdk .ot-pc-footer .ot-btn-container button.save-preference-btn-handler {
    background-color: #fff !important;
    border: 2px solid #76B900;
    font-size: 16px;
    padding: 11px 13px !important;
    letter-spacing: inherit;
}

#onetrust-pc-sdk .ot-pc-footer .ot-btn-container button.ot-pc-refuse-all-handler:hover,
#onetrust-pc-sdk .ot-pc-footer .ot-btn-container button.save-preference-btn-handler:hover {
    border: 2px solid #000 !important;
    opacity: 1;
}

#onetrust-pc-sdk .ot-accordion-layout.ot-cat-item .ot-category-desc,
#onetrust-consent-sdk #onetrust-pc-sdk h4.ot-cat-header,
#onetrust-pc-sdk .ot-host-opt li>div div,
#onetrust-pc-sdk #ot-host-lst .ot-host-name, 
#onetrust-pc-sdk #ot-host-lst .ot-host-name a {
    font-size: 15px;   
    color: #000000;
}

#onetrust-consent-sdk #onetrust-pc-sdk *:focus,
#onetrust-consent-sdk #onetrust-pc-sdk .ot-vlst-cntr > a:focus {
    outline-width: 0px;  
}

#onetrust-banner-sdk :not(.ot-leg-btn-container)>button:not(.ot-link-btn):focus,

#onetrust-pc-sdk :not(.ot-leg-btn-container)>button:not(.ot-link-btn):hover,
#onetrust-pc-sdk :not(.ot-leg-btn-container)>button:not(.ot-link-btn):focus,

}
#ot-fltr-cnt .ot-fltr-btns #filter-apply-handler,

border: 2px solid #76b900;
}

  <div class="entry-content">
<p>NVIDIA CUDA 13.1 introduces the largest and most comprehensive update to the CUDA platform since it was invented two decades ago. </p>



<p>In this release, you’ll find new features and updates for improving performance and driving accelerated computing, including:</p>



<ul class="wp-block-list">
<li>The launch of <a href="https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/focus-on-your-algorithm-nvidia-cuda-tile-handles-the-hardware" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">NVIDIA CUDA Tile</a>, our tile-based programming model for abstracting away specialized hardware, including tensor cores.</li>



<li>Runtime API exposure of green contexts.</li>



<li>Emulation for double and single precisions in NVIDIA cuBLAS.</li>



<li>A completely rewritten <a href="https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-programming-guide/index.html" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">CUDA programming guide</a>, designed for both novice and advanced CUDA programmers.</li>
</ul>



<h2 id="cuda_tile_programming" class="wp-block-heading">CUDA Tile programming<a href="#cuda_tile_programming" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h2>



<p>To help create software for current and future GPUs, NVIDIA CUDA 13.1 is launching <a href="https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda/tile" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">CUDA Tile</a>, which enables you to write GPU kernels at a layer above SIMT. Currently, in SIMT programming, you specify kernels by partitioning data and defining each thread’s path of execution. Using CUDA Tile, you can bring your code up a layer and specify chunks of data called tiles. You specify the mathematical operations to be executed on those tiles, and the compiler and runtime determine the best way to launch that work onto individual threads. The tile model abstracts away the details of using specialized hardware such as tensor cores, and your tile code will be compatible with future GPU architectures.</p>



<p>CUDA 13.1 is releasing two components for tile programming.</p>



<ul class="wp-block-list">
<li><a href="https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/tile-ir" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">CUDA Tile IR</a>: A new virtual instruction set architecture (ISA) for programming NVIDIA GPUS.</li>



<li><a href="https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cutile-python" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">cuTile Python</a>: A new domain-specific language (DSL) for authoring array and tile-based kernels in Python.</li>
</ul>



<p>In this first version of the software:</p>



<ul class="wp-block-list">
<li>CUDA tile is supported on NVIDIA Blackwell (compute capability 10.x and 12.x) products only. Future versions of CUDA will add support for more architectures.</li>



<li>We’ve focused our development efforts on tile programming for AI algorithms. In future releases of CUDA, we’ll continue to add more features, functionality, and performance.</li>



<li>In an upcoming CUDA release, we plan to introduce an implementation in C++. </li>
</ul>



<p>Check out more information about CUDA Tile IR and <a href="https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/simplify-gpu-programming-with-nvidia-cuda-tile-in-python" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">cuTile Python</a>.</p>



<h2 id="cuda_software_updates" class="wp-block-heading">CUDA software updates<a href="#cuda_software_updates" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h2>



<p>Here are some other important software updates included in this release of CUDA.</p>



<h3 id="runtime_exposure_of_green_contexts " class="wp-block-heading">Runtime exposure of green contexts <a href="#runtime_exposure_of_green_contexts " class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>Green contexts in CUDA are a lightweight alternative to traditional CUDA contexts, designed to provide developers with a mechanism for finer-grained spatial partitioning and resource provisioning on the GPU. They’ve been available in the driver API since CUDA 12.4, and starting now, green contexts are available in the runtime API. </p>



<p>Green contexts enable you to define and manage distinct partitions of GPU resources, primarily Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs), and dedicate a specific set of SMs to a particular context. You can then launch CUDA kernels and manage streams that run only within the resources provisioned for that green context. A typical example is when your application has latency-sensitive code with priority over all other GPU work. By allocating SM resources to a dedicated green context for this code, and the rest to another green context for other code, you guarantee available SMs ready for this computation.</p>



<p>CUDA 13.1 also introduces a more customizable <code>split()</code> API. Developers can build SM partitions that previously required multiple API calls and the ability to configure work queues to minimize false dependencies between work submitted in different green contexts. </p>



<p>The <a href="https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cuda-programming-guide/04-special-topics/green-contexts.html" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">CUDA programming guide</a> has more about these features and the runtime exposure of green contexts.</p>



<h2 id="cuda_multi-process_service_updates" class="wp-block-heading">CUDA Multi-Process Service updates<a href="#cuda_multi-process_service_updates" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h2>



<p>CUDA 13.1 brings new features and functionality to Multi-Process Service (MPS). For complete information on these new features, please see the <a href="https://docs.nvidia.com/deploy/mps/index.html" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">MPS documentation</a>. A few of the highlights include:</p>



<h3 id="memory_locality_optimization_partition " class="wp-block-heading">Memory locality optimization partition <a href="#memory_locality_optimization_partition " class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>Memory locality optimization partition (MLOPart) is a feature on some NVIDIA Blackwell (compute capability 10.0 and 10.3) and newer GPUs. Users can create specialized CUDA devices optimized for improving memory locality. MLOPart devices are derived from a single underlying GPU but present as multiple devices with fewer compute resources and less available memory. Compute capability 10.0 and 10.3 GPUs each have two partitions.</p>



<p>When using MLOPart on supported GPUs, each partition appears as a distinct CUDA device, with associated compute and memory resources. Currently, MLOPart is only supported on NVIDIA B200 and NVIDIA B300 products, and a future release of CUDA will support the NVIDIA GB200 and NVIDIA GB300 products.</p>



<h3 id="static_streaming_multiprocessor_partitioning" class="wp-block-heading">Static streaming multiprocessor partitioning<a href="#static_streaming_multiprocessor_partitioning" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>As an alternative to the current dynamic execution resource provisioning available in MPS, static streaming multiprocessor (SM) partitioning is a feature for NVIDIA Ampere architecture (compute capability 8.0) and newer GPUs that provides a way to create exclusive SM partitions for MPS clients. </p>



<p>This mode is enabled by launching the MPS control daemon with the <code>-S</code> or <code>--static-partitioning</code> flag, and its main purpose is to deliver deterministic resource allocation and improved isolation between MPS clients. The fundamental unit of partitioning is a “chunk,” which varies in size based on the GPU architecture—for example, 8 SMs on Hopper (compute capability 9.0) and newer discrete GPUs.</p>



<h3 id="emulation_for_double_and_single_precisions_in_cublas " class="wp-block-heading">Emulation for double and single precisions in cuBLAS <a href="#emulation_for_double_and_single_precisions_in_cublas " class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>While not strictly a CUDA 13.1 update, the cuBLAS update in NVIDIA CUDA Toolkit 13.0 introduced new APIs and implementations for boosting the performance of double-precision (FP64) matrix multiplications (matmuls). This is achieved through floating-point (FP) emulation on Tensor Cores found in GPU architectures such as NVIDIA GB200 NVL72, and NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition. For comprehensive information on GPU compatibility for both FP32 and FP64 emulation, refer to the <a href="https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cublas/#floating-point-emulation-support-overview" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">cuBLAS documentation</a>. </p>



<h2 id="developer_tools" class="wp-block-heading">Developer tools<a href="#developer_tools" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h2>



<p>Developer tools are a crucial part of the CUDA platform. This release delivers several innovations and feature enhancements, including:</p>



<h3 id="cuda_tile_kernel_profiling" class="wp-block-heading">CUDA Tile kernel profiling<a href="#cuda_tile_kernel_profiling" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>NVIDIA Nsight Compute 2025.4 adds support for profiling CUDA Tile kernels. Updates include a new “Result Type” column on the summary page for denoting Tile vs. SIMT kernels. A new “Tile Statistics” section on the details page summarizes Tile dimensions and utilization of important pipelines. The source page also supports mapping metrics to the high-level cuTile kernel source.</p>

<p>This Nsight Compute release also adds support for profiling CUDA graph nodes from device-launched graphs and source page navigation improvements with clickable label links for both compiler-generated and user-generated labels.</p>



<h3 id="compile-time_patching" class="wp-block-heading">Compile-time patching<a href="#compile-time_patching" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>NVIDIA Compute Sanitizer 2025.4 adds support for NVIDIA CUDA Compiler <strong>(</strong>NVCC) compile-time patching through the <code>-fdevice-sanitize=memcheck</code> compiler flag. This patching enhances memory error detection and improves compute sanitizer performance. </p>



<p>Compile-time instrumentation integrates error detection directly into NVCC for faster runs while catching more subtle memory issues, such as illegal accesses between adjacent allocations, through advanced base-and-bounds analysis. This means you can debug memory problems without sacrificing speed, run more tests, and maintain productivity. Right now, only memcheck is supported.</p>



<p>To use this new feature, compile your code with the NVCC flag as follows</p>


<div class="wp-block-syntaxhighlighter-code "><div><div id="highlighter_570589" class="syntaxhighlighter nogutter  bash"><table border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0"><tbody><tr><td class="code"><div class="container"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2"><code class="bash plain">nvcc -fdevice-sanitize=memcheck -o myapp myapp.cu</code></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table></div></div></div>


<p>Then run your application with compute-sanitizer using the memcheck tool.</p>


<div class="wp-block-syntaxhighlighter-code "><div><div id="highlighter_271386" class="syntaxhighlighter nogutter  bash"><table border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0"><tbody><tr><td class="code"><div class="container"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2"><code class="bash plain">compute-sanitizer --tool memcheck myapp</code></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table></div></div></div>


<p>For complete information on compile-time patching, refer to the compute-sanitizer <a href="https://docs.nvidia.com/compute-sanitizer/index.html#compile-time-patching" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">documentation</a>.</p>



<h2 id="nvidia_nsight_systems" class="wp-block-heading">NVIDIA Nsight Systems<a href="#nvidia_nsight_systems" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h2>



<p>NVIDIA Nsight Systems 2025.6.1 <a href="https://developer.nvidia.com/nsight-systems/get-started" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">releases</a> concurrently with CUDA Toolkit 13.1, with several new tracing features, including:</p>



<ul class="wp-block-list">
<li>System-wide CUDA trace: <code>--cuda-trace-scope</code> enables tracing across process trees or the entire system.</li>



<li>CUDA host function trace: Added trace support for CUDA Graph host function nodes and <code>cudaLaunchHostFunc()</code>, which executes on the host and blocks the stream.</li>



<li>CUDA hardware trace: Hardware-based tracing is now the default when supported; use<code> --trace=cuda-sw</code> to revert to software mode.</li>



<li>Green context timeline rows now show SM allocation in tooltips to help users understand GPU resource utilization.</li>
</ul>



<h2 id="math_libraries" class="wp-block-heading">Math libraries<a href="#math_libraries" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h2>



<p>New features across our core CUDA Toolkit math libraries include:</p>



<ul class="wp-block-list">
<li><strong>NVIDIA cuBLAS</strong>: A new experimental API with Grouped GEMM supports FP8 and BF16/FP16 for Blackwell GPUs. Grouped GEMMs for the noted datatypes with CUDA Graph support provides a host-synchronization-free implementation with device-side shapes for up to <strong>4x</strong> speed-up over a multi-stream GEMM implementation in the MoE use case.</li>



<li><strong>NVIDIA cuSPARSE</strong>: A new sparse matrix vector multiplication (SpMVOp) API with improved performance compared to the CsrMV API. This API supports CSR format, 32-bit index, double precision, and user-defined epilogues.</li>



<li><strong>NVIDIA cuFFT</strong>: A new set of APIs, called cuFFT device API, provides host functions for querying or generating device function code and database metadata in a C++ header file. Designed for the cuFFTDx library, it facilitates the generation of cuFFTDx code blocks by querying cuFFT, which can be linked with the cuFFTDx application to improve performance.</li>
</ul>



<p>Performance updates on new Blackwell architectures are available. Select updates for key APIs, and performance follows. </p>

<h3 id="cusolver_blackwell_performance" class="wp-block-heading">cuSOLVER Blackwell performance<a href="#cusolver_blackwell_performance" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>CUDA 13.1 continues to improve batched SYEVD and GEEV APIs for eigen-decomposition, delivering performance enhancements.</p>



<p>Batched SYEV (cusolverDnXsyevBatched) is a uniform batched version of cuSOLVER’s SYEV routine, computing eigenvalues and eigenvectors for symmetric/Hermitian matrices, ideal for parallel solving of many small matrices.</p>



<p>Figure 3 shows tests on a batch size of 5,000 (24-256 rows) with about a 2x speedup on the NVIDIA Blackwell RTX Pro 6000 Server Edition compared to the NVIDIA L40S, correlating with expected memory throughput increases.</p>

<h2 id="nvidia_cuda_core_compute_libraries" class="wp-block-heading">NVIDIA CUDA Core Compute Libraries<a href="#nvidia_cuda_core_compute_libraries" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h2>



<p>NVIDIA CUDA Core Compute Libraries (CCCL) features several innovations and enhancements for <a href="https://nvidia.github.io/cccl/cub/" data-wpel-link="external" target="_blank" rel="follow">CUB</a>.</p>



<h3 id="deterministic_floating-point_reductions" class="wp-block-heading">Deterministic floating-point reductions<a href="#deterministic_floating-point_reductions" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>Due to the non-associativity of floating point addition, <code>cub::DeviceReduce</code> historically only guaranteed bitwise-identical results run-to-run on the same GPU. This was implemented as a two-pass algorithm.</p>



<p><a href="https://github.com/NVIDIA/cccl/releases/tag/v3.1.0" data-wpel-link="external" target="_blank" rel="follow">NVIDIA CCCL 3.1</a>, part of CUDA 13.1, provides two additional floating-point determinism options for you to make trade-offs between determinism and performance. </p>



<ul class="wp-block-list">
<li><strong>Not-guaranteed</strong>: Single-pass reduction using atomics. This isn’t guaranteed to provide bitwise-identical results.</li>



<li><strong>GPU-to-GPU: </strong>Based on reproducible reduction in Kate Clark’s NVIDIA <a href="https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/on-demand/session/gtc24-s62405/" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">GTC 2024 talk. </a>Results are always bitwise-identical.</li>
</ul>



<p>The determinism option can be set through a flag, as shown in the following code.</p>


<div class="wp-block-syntaxhighlighter-code "><div><div id="highlighter_162523" class="syntaxhighlighter nogutter  cpp"><table border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0"><tbody><tr><td class="code"><div class="container"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2"><code class="cpp comments">// Pick your desired trade-off of performance and determinism</code></div><div class="line number2 index1 alt1"><code class="cpp comments">// auto env = cuda::execution::require(cuda::execution::determinism::not_guaranteed);</code></div><div class="line number3 index2 alt2"><code class="cpp comments">// auto env = cuda::execution::require(cuda::execution::determinism::run_to_run);</code></div><div class="line number4 index3 alt1"><code class="cpp comments">// auto env = cuda::execution::require(cuda::execution::determinism::gpu_to_gpu);</code></div><div class="line number5 index4 alt2"><code class="cpp plain">cub::DeviceReduce::Sum(..., env);</code></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table></div></div></div>

<h3 id="more_convenient_single-phase_cub_apis" class="wp-block-heading">More convenient single-phase CUB APIs<a href="#more_convenient_single-phase_cub_apis" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h3>



<p>Nearly every <a href="https://nvidia.github.io/cccl/cub/" data-wpel-link="external" target="_blank" rel="follow">CUB</a> algorithm requires temporary storage for intermediate scratch space. Historically, users had to query and allocate the necessary temporary storage through a two-phase call pattern that is cumbersome and error-prone if arguments aren’t passed the same between two invocations. </p>



<p>CCCL 3.1 adds new overloads to some CUB algorithms that accept a <strong>memory resource,</strong> so you skip the temp-storage query/allocate/free pattern. </p>



<div class="wp-block-columns is-layout-flex wp-container-core-columns-is-layout-9d6595d7 wp-block-columns-is-layout-flex">
<div class="wp-block-column is-layout-flow wp-block-column-is-layout-flow">
<p><strong>Before (two-phase)</strong></p>


<div class="wp-block-syntaxhighlighter-code "><div><div id="highlighter_622610" class="syntaxhighlighter nogutter  plain"><table border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0"><tbody><tr><td class="code"><div class="container"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2"><code class="plain plain">// determine temporary storage size</code></div><div class="line number2 index1 alt1"><code class="plain plain">cub::DeviceScan::ExclusiveSum(d_temp_storage, </code></div><div class="line number3 index2 alt2"><code class="plain spaces">               </code><code class="plain plain">temp_storage_bytes, </code></div><div class="line number4 index3 alt1"><code class="plain spaces">               </code><code class="plain plain">nullptr, ...);</code></div><div class="line number5 index4 alt2"><code class="plain spaces"> </code> </div><div class="line number6 index5 alt1"><code class="plain plain">// Allocate the required temporary storage</code></div><div class="line number7 index6 alt2"><code class="plain plain">cudaMallocAsync(&d_temp_storage,</code></div><div class="line number8 index7 alt1"><code class="plain spaces">        </code><code class="plain plain">temp_storage_bytes, stream);</code></div><div class="line number9 index8 alt2"><code class="plain spaces"> </code> </div><div class="line number10 index9 alt1"><code class="plain plain">// run the actual scan</code></div><div class="line number11 index10 alt2"><code class="plain plain">cub::DeviceScan::ExclusiveSum(d_temp_storage,</code></div><div class="line number12 index11 alt1"><code class="plain spaces">               </code><code class="plain plain">temp_storage_bytes, </code></div><div class="line number13 index12 alt2"><code class="plain spaces">               </code><code class="plain plain">d_input...);</code></div><div class="line number14 index13 alt1"> </div><div class="line number15 index14 alt2"><code class="plain spaces"> </code><code class="plain plain">// Free the temporary storage</code></div><div class="line number16 index15 alt1"><code class="plain plain">cudaFreeAsync(temp_storage, stream);</code></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table></div></div></div></div>



<div class="wp-block-column is-layout-flow wp-block-column-is-layout-flow">
<p><strong>After (single-phase)</strong></p>


<div class="wp-block-syntaxhighlighter-code "><div><div id="highlighter_360269" class="syntaxhighlighter nogutter  plain"><table border="0" cellpadding="0" cellspacing="0"><tbody><tr><td class="code"><div class="container"><div class="line number1 index0 alt2"><code class="plain plain">// Pool mr uses cudaMallocAsync under the hood</code></div><div class="line number2 index1 alt1"><code class="plain plain">cuda::device_memory_pool mr{cuda::devices[0]};</code></div><div class="line number3 index2 alt2"> </div><div class="line number4 index3 alt1"><code class="plain plain">// Single call. Temp storage is handled by the pool.</code></div><div class="line number5 index4 alt2"><code class="plain plain">cub::DeviceScan::ExclusiveSum(d_input,..., mr);</code></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table></div></div></div></div>
</div>



<h2 id="learn_more" class="wp-block-heading">Learn more<a href="#learn_more" class="heading-anchor-link"><i class="fas fa-link"></i></a></h2>



<p>The release of CUDA 13.1 brings many new features and ushers in a new era of GPU programming with CUDA Tile. Check out <a href="https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda/tile" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">CUDA Tile</a> resources, <a href="https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads" data-wpel-link="internal" target="_self" rel="follow">download CUDA Toolkit 13.1</a>, and get started today.</p>

      const defaultLinks = JSON.parse("{\n    \"columns\": [],\n    \"links\": [\n      {\n        \"text\": \"Privacy Policy\",\n        \"href\": \"https:\/\/www.nvidia.com\/en-us\/about-nvidia\/privacy-policy\/\"\n      },\n      {\n        \"text\": \"Your Privacy Choices\",\n        \"href\": \"https:\/\/www.nvidia.com\/en-us\/about-nvidia\/privacy-center\/\"\n      },\n      {\n        \"text\": \"Terms of Use\",\n        \"href\": \"https:\/\/developer.nvidia.com\/legal\/terms\"\n      },\n      {\n        \"text\": \"Accessibility\",\n        \"href\": \"https:\/\/www.nvidia.com\/en-us\/about-nvidia\/accessibility\/\"\n      },\n      {\n        \"text\": \"Corporate Policies\",\n        \"href\": \"https:\/\/www.nvidia.com\/en-us\/about-nvidia\/company-policies\/\"\n      },\n      {\n        \"text\": \"Contact\",\n        \"href\": \"https:\/\/developer.nvidia.com\/contact\"\n      }\n    ],\n    \"news\": {\n      \"title\": \"Sign up for NVIDIA News\",\n      \"href\": \"https:\/\/developer.nvidia.com\/email-signup\"\n    },\n    \"social\": {\n      \"title\": \"Follow NVIDIA Developer\",\n      \"links\": [\n        {\n          \"text\": \"Facebook\",\n          \"href\": \"https:\/\/facebook.com\/nvidiaai\",\n          \"provider\": \"facebook\"\n        },\n        {\n          \"text\": \"Instagram\",\n          \"href\": \"https:\/\/instagram.com\/nvidiadeveloper\",\n          \"provider\": \"instagram\"\n        },\n        {\n          \"text\": \"LinkedIn\",\n          \"href\": \"https:\/\/www.linkedin.com\/showcase\/nvidia-ai\",\n          \"provider\": \"linkedin\"\n        },\n        {\n          \"text\": \"Twitter\",\n          \"href\": \"https:\/\/twitter.com\/nvidiadeveloper\",\n          \"provider\": \"twitter\"\n        },\n        {\n          \"text\": \"YouTube\",\n          \"href\": \"https:\/\/www.youtube.com\/user\/NVIDIADeveloper\",\n          \"provider\": \"youtube\"\n        }\n      ]\n    }\n  }\n\n")

      const mountFooterComponent = (locale, response) => {
        new NVDeveloperFooter({
          target: document.getElementById('nv-footer'),
          props: {
            locale: locale,
            menu: response
          }
        })
      }

      const handleError = (error, locale, menu) => {
        console.debug(error)
        mountFooterComponent(locale, menu)
      }

      const initFooter = () => {
        let menuUrl = 'https://d29g4g2dyqv443.cloudfront.net/menu/en-US/footer.json'
                let locale = 'en-US';
        
        if (!menuUrl) {
          handleError('Footer menu URL not defined.', locale, defaultLinks)
          return
        }

        fetch(menuUrl)
          .then((response) => response.json())
          .then((response) => mountFooterComponent(locale, response))
          .catch((error) => handleError(error, locale, defaultLinks))
      }

      document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', initFooter);
    })();
  </script>

var metaslider_56284 = function($) {
            $('#metaslider_56284').flexslider({ 
                slideshowSpeed:3000,
                animation:"fade",
                controlNav:false,
                directionNav:false,
                pauseOnHover:true,
                direction:"horizontal",
                reverse:false,
                keyboard:true,
                touch:true,
                animationSpeed:600,
                prevText:"<",
                nextText:">",
                smoothHeight:false,
                fadeFirstSlide:false,
                slideshow:true,
                pausePlay:false,
                showPlayText:false,
                playText:false,
                pauseText:false,
                start: function(slider) {
                
                // Function to disable focusable elements in aria-hidden slides
                function disableAriaHiddenFocusableElements() {
                    var slider_ = $('#metaslider_56284');
                    
                    // Disable focusable elements in slides with aria-hidden='true'
                    slider_.find('.slides li[aria-hidden="true"] a, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] button, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] input, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] select, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] textarea, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] [tabindex]:not([tabindex="-1"])').attr('tabindex', '-1');
                    
                    // Disable focusable elements in cloned slides (these should never be focusable)
                    slider_.find('.slides li.clone a, .slides li.clone button, .slides li.clone input, .slides li.clone select, .slides li.clone textarea, .slides li.clone [tabindex]:not([tabindex="-1"])').attr('tabindex', '-1');
                }
                
                // Initial setup
                disableAriaHiddenFocusableElements();
                
                // Observer for aria-hidden and clone changes
                if (typeof MutationObserver !== 'undefined') {
                    var ariaObserver = new MutationObserver(function(mutations) {
                        var shouldUpdate = false;
                        mutations.forEach(function(mutation) {
                            if (mutation.type === 'attributes' && mutation.attributeName === 'aria-hidden') {
                                shouldUpdate = true;
                            }
                            if (mutation.type === 'childList') {
                                // Check if cloned slides were added/removed
                                for (var i = 0; i < mutation.addedNodes.length; i++) {
                                    if (mutation.addedNodes[i].nodeType === 1 && 
                                        (mutation.addedNodes[i].classList.contains('clone') || 
                                         mutation.addedNodes[i].querySelector && mutation.addedNodes[i].querySelector('.clone'))) {
                                        shouldUpdate = true;
                                        break;
                                    }
                                }
                            }
                        });
                        if (shouldUpdate) {
                            setTimeout(disableAriaHiddenFocusableElements, 10);
                        }
                    });
                    
                    var targetNode = $('#metaslider_56284')[0];
                    if (targetNode) {
                        ariaObserver.observe(targetNode, { 
                            attributes: true, 
                            attributeFilter: ['aria-hidden'],
                            childList: true,
                            subtree: true
                        });
                    }
                }
                
                },
                after: function(slider) {
                
                // Re-disable focusable elements after slide transitions
                var slider_ = $('#metaslider_56284');
                
                // Disable focusable elements in slides with aria-hidden='true'
                slider_.find('.slides li[aria-hidden="true"] a, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] button, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] input, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] select, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] textarea, .slides li[aria-hidden="true"] [tabindex]:not([tabindex="-1"])').attr('tabindex', '-1');
                
                // Disable focusable elements in cloned slides
                slider_.find('.slides li.clone a, .slides li.clone button, .slides li.clone input, .slides li.clone select, .slides li.clone textarea, .slides li.clone [tabindex]:not([tabindex="-1"])').attr('tabindex', '-1');
                
                }
            });
            $(document).trigger('metaslider/initialized', '#metaslider_56284');
        };

To opt-out of the "sale" and "sharing" of personal information through cookies, you must opt-out of optional cookies using the toggles below. You cannot opt out of Required Cookies as they are deployed to ensure the proper functioning of our website (such as prompting the cookie banner and remembering your settings, etc.). By clicking "Save and Accept" or "Decline All" at the bottom, you consent to the use of cookies and other tools as described in our <a href="https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/about-nvidia/cookie-policy/" target="_self">Cookie Policy</a> in accordance with your settings and accept our <a href="https://www.nvidia.com/en-us/about-nvidia/terms-of-service/" target="_self">Terms of Service</a> (which contains important waivers).
